pmos

Mentoring & Coaching PMs — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · PM Tactics — Leadership. Developing other PMs is the highest-leverage work a product leader does — Cagan's EMPOWERED is blunt that "developing people is job 1." The field splits the work into three distinct modes that get conflated at everyone's peril: mentoring (relationship-based career perspective), coaching (skill-building toward competence), and managing (accountability for results). The coaching toolkit is well-canonized: the facilitator stance of asking over telling, the GROW model for structured conversations, Cagan's assessment-and-coaching-plan built on a product/process/people gap analysis, and feedback frameworks — SBI inside Radical Candor — delivered through the weekly 1:1. Growth itself is mapped against career ladders and competency frameworks, and the leader's endgame is building a team's capability, not just one PM's. Provenance: practitioner-canon synthesis. 16 verified sources.

What developing PMs means

Developing PMs is the deliberate work of growing another product manager's capability — their judgment, skills, and confidence — over time, as distinct from simply getting this quarter's roadmap shipped. The practitioner framing is that it starts with understanding "each individual's strengths and gaps through discovery," then building "personalized development plans" that balance concrete skill-building with broader leadership growth. It is a portfolio of activities, not a single act: assessment, feedback, structured conversations, growth planning, and the occasional well-timed stretch assignment.

The reason it deserves its own treatment is that PM growth is unusually non-linear. The career path runs from APM through CPO with expectations that "intensify" at each stage — from learning customer empathy and basic prioritization, to strategic ownership, to executive vision-setting. What makes a great APM (flawless execution) is not what makes a great Senior PM (strategic judgment), so developing a PM means actively reshaping what they optimize for as they climb — not just sharpening what they already do. For a senior PM or product leader, this primer is the toolkit for doing that reshaping deliberately rather than by osmosis.

Why it is a leader's job one

The strongest claim in the canon is that developing people is not one of a product leader's duties — it is the duty. Cagan's EMPOWERED states it flatly: "developing people is job 1," and the entire book reframes the manager-of-PMs role around coaching rather than reviewing output. The same source notes that a leader's "primary responsibility is coaching," which is a deliberate inversion of the default manager instinct to spend time in status reviews and decision approvals. The argument is leverage: a leader who improves a PM's judgment improves every future decision that PM makes, whereas a leader who makes the decision for them improves exactly one.

This reframing has a sharp implication that senior PMs moving into leadership routinely underweight: the skills that made you a great PM are not the skills that make you a great developer of PMs. The job shifts from being the best decision-maker in the room to building the people who will make those decisions. The recurring failure — staying the hero PM who swoops in — is so common precisely because it feels productive in the moment while quietly capping the team at the leader's own bandwidth. Treating development as job one is the structural fix: it forces the leader's time toward the activity with compounding returns.

Mentoring vs. coaching vs. managing

The single most useful distinction in this field is the three-way split between mentoring, coaching, and managing — three modes that are constantly conflated and that call for different postures. Mentoring is "ongoing perspective, support, and guidance via an established relationship," oriented toward broader career development rather than real-time skill feedback; it is typically informal, often free, and frequently sits outside the reporting line. Coaching, by contrast, "focuses on personal growth and guiding better decisions over time" through more structured, skill-building work — it is the targeted development of specific competencies.

The third mode, managing, is what the other two are often mistaken for: managing carries accountability for results and the formal authority to direct work. The senior-PM nuance is that a manager-of-PMs occupies all three roles at once and must consciously switch between them — and the most damaging error is letting the managing mode (with its evaluative, results-accountable stance) bleed into a coaching conversation, which turns a developmental moment into a performance review and shuts the report down. The mentoring literature is explicit that conflating mentoring with coaching is a named pitfall. Naming which mode you are in — out loud, at the start of a conversation — is the practical discipline that keeps them separate.

The coaching stance: asking, not telling

The foundational move in coaching — the one everything else depends on — is the shift from giving answers to drawing them out. Google's re:Work frames it as a manager moving "from 'fixers' to 'facilitators'," practicing "active listening and ask[ing] open-ended questions to facilitate the team member's own insight" rather than supplying the solution. The rationale is not gentleness; it is efficacy. A PM who reaches a conclusion themselves internalizes the reasoning and can reproduce it next time, whereas a PM handed the answer has learned only that the boss has answers.

For senior PMs this is genuinely hard, because the whole reason you were promoted is that you usually do know the answer, and watching someone work toward it slowly is uncomfortable. The discipline is to treat your own correct answer as a last resort rather than an opening move — to ask "what have you considered?" and "what would happen if…?" before offering "here's what I'd do." This stance is also what cleanly separates coaching from managing: the manager mode optimizes for the right decision now; the coaching mode optimizes for the PM's ability to make the right decision unaided later. The asking-not-telling posture is the behavioral signature of being in the coaching mode at all.

The GROW model

The most widely-used structure for a coaching conversation is GROW, an acronym that gives the asking-not-telling stance a repeatable shape. The four phases are Goal ("what an individual is looking to achieve"), Reality ("the facts of the current situation"), Options/Obstacles ("all the different ways you can address the situation"), and Will/Way-forward ("how you will choose to act"). The manager walks the report through each phase with questions, so the conversation moves from aspiration to honest situation assessment to generated options to a concrete commitment — all owned by the report.

GROW's power is that it imposes order on what otherwise becomes either an aimless venting session or a disguised lecture. Applied in 1:1s, managers "ask reflective questions rather than providing solutions," so reports "discover their own path forward and build ownership." Two senior-PM cautions are worth flagging. First, the model's most-skipped phase is Reality — coaches rush from Goal to Options without honestly establishing where things actually stand, and the resulting plan floats free of facts. Second, GROW is a structure for a coaching conversation, not a script to recite; the value is in the sequence of attention (goal before reality before options before commitment), not in announcing the letters.

Cagan's assessment and coaching plan

Where GROW structures a single conversation, Cagan's EMPOWERED supplies the structure for a PM's whole development arc. The method assesses each person across three pillars — "product, process, and people skills" — to locate capability gaps against the expectations of their role. This is a deliberate widening of the lens: a PM can be strong on product craft yet weak on the people skills (influence, communication) that gate the next level, and an assessment across all three pillars surfaces exactly where the binding constraint is.

The output is a focused plan, and the focus is the point. The coaching plan targets "a few critical areas where improvement is needed, rather than attempting broad transformation" — Cagan's specific guidance is to limit it to the three areas with the largest gap between expectation and current capability. The senior-PM discipline here is twofold: first, gap analysis against role expectations (not against an abstract ideal of a perfect PM), so the plan is calibrated to the level the person is growing into; and second, ruthless prioritization to three areas, because a development plan that lists ten things is a development plan the PM will action on zero. Concentration of effort beats breadth.

Giving effective feedback

Feedback is the raw material of development, and the canon has converged on two complementary frameworks. The structural one is SBI. The Center for Creative Leadership's model frames feedback as Situation, Behavior, and Impact — anchoring on "concrete observations rather than assumptions about intent," which "reduces anxiety around giving feedback, as well as the defensiveness of the recipient." Applied to PMs, SBI's value is that it strips out the character judgment ("you're disorganized") that triggers defensiveness and replaces it with a reviewable sequence of observation.

The relational frame around the structure is Kim Scott's Radical Candor. Its two principles are "Care Personally" and "Challenge Directly," and notably Scott's own playbook recommends using "the SBI model" to deliver the challenge, along with HHIIPP — "helpful, humble, immediate, in-person, public praise/private criticism, and focusing on behaviors rather than personality." The two frameworks are not competitors but layers: Radical Candor sets the relationship (you challenge directly because you care personally), and SBI provides the words. The senior-PM failure mode the pairing guards against is "ruinous empathy" in Scott's terms — caring so much about the relationship that you withhold the direct challenge, which feels kind and is actually a failure to develop the person.

The 1:1 as the core instrument

All of these tools are delivered through one recurring container: the one-on-one. EMPOWERED's prescription is "regular weekly 1-on-1s (minimum 30 minutes) focused on growth rather than status updates." That last clause is the whole argument — a 1:1 spent on project status is a meeting the leader could have gotten from a dashboard, while a 1:1 spent on the PM's growth is the irreplaceable developmental act. The cadence (weekly) and the protected duration (30+ minutes) are not bureaucratic detail; they are what make development continuous rather than an annual-review event.

The 1:1 is also where the other frameworks land in practice. The GROW model is explicitly "applied in 1:1s," SBI feedback is delivered there ("private criticism," per Radical Candor's HHIIPP), and the coaching plan's three focus areas are revisited there. For senior PMs newly managing, the most common and most corrosive mistake is letting the 1:1 default to status — because status is concrete, urgent, and easy, while coaching is abstract, important, and hard. Defending the 1:1 as growth time, against the constant gravitational pull of status, is arguably the single highest-leverage habit in this entire primer.

Growth plans and career ladders

Development needs a map of where the PM is going, and that map is the career ladder. The PM ladder typically splits into IC and management tracks across "roughly 5–9 levels from APM through VP," with the focus shifting "from execution at junior levels to vision at senior levels." The ladder matters for development because it makes expectations legible: a PM cannot close a gap to the next level if no one has named what that level actually requires, and a written ladder converts vague "get more strategic" advice into specific, assessable competencies per rung.

The non-obvious senior-PM point is that the IC/management fork around Senior PM is a genuine branch, not a hierarchy. The two tracks diverge into deep expertise and influence (Staff/Principal) versus people leadership and org design (Director+), and a good growth plan respects which track the person actually wants rather than defaulting everyone toward management. The practical instrument is to pair the ladder with the assessment: locate the PM on the ladder, identify the competencies the next rung demands, and let those gaps populate the coaching plan's focus areas. Ladder plus assessment plus coaching plan is the closed loop that turns "I want to grow" into a concrete quarter of development.

Competency frameworks

To assess a PM you need a vocabulary of what PM skill is, and the most-cited modern one is Ravi Mehta's. His framework defines twelve competencies across four areas: "Product Execution, Customer Insight, Product Strategy, and Influencing People." The four-area split is useful precisely because it spans the craft (execution, customer insight) and the leadership (strategy, influence) dimensions, mirroring Cagan's product/process/people lens and giving a coach a structured grid to assess against rather than a gut feel.

The framework's most important claim is about balance, not mastery. Mehta argues peak PMs "don't excel equally at all competencies; rather, they identify their strengths, understand gaps, and build balanced teams with complementary skills." This reframes development away from making every PM a uniform all-rounder and toward helping each PM sharpen their spike while shoring up disqualifying weaknesses. The coaching literature agrees that "having a balanced set of skills in product management is crucial" — but the balance often lives at the team level, not within every individual. The senior-PM application: use the competency grid to assess, but coach toward a distinctive strength rather than a flat average.

Building team capability and scaling

The leader's true unit of development is not the individual PM but the team's collective capability. Scaling guidance stresses establishing "clear ownership by dividing responsibilities by product, role, or domain expertise" and, crucially, hiring "a mix of experience levels and complementary skill sets rather than cloning the original PM." The complementary-skills point connects directly to the competency framework: if peak PMs spike rather than round out, a strong team is deliberately assembled so its members' spikes cover one another's gaps.

Scaling also changes the development work qualitatively. The same source notes "what worked with one product team breaks down as organizations grow," and that "high PM turnover often indicates development and support gaps" — i.e., a retention problem is frequently a coaching problem in disguise. As a team grows, the leader shifts from coaching every PM personally to building the system of development: leveling structures, onboarding, a culture where senior PMs coach juniors, and supporting roles (analysts, ops) that free PMs for higher-judgment work. The senior-PM endgame is to make development scale beyond your own calendar — to build a team that grows its own people.

Finding and being a mentor

Not all development happens inside the reporting line, and mentoring is the mode that operates outside it. A good mentor "offers firsthand wisdom from their experiences, helping you navigate relationships and the discipline of product management faster than learning alone" — value that a manager, constrained by the evaluative relationship, often cannot provide. This is why even a well-coached PM benefits from mentors: a mentor can be candid about career moves, political dynamics, and self-doubt in ways a PM may never risk with the person who writes their review.

For senior PMs the relationship runs both ways. To find a mentor, the guidance is to "look within your company first, participate in product communities, build genuine relationships based on mutual benefit," and demonstrate you are "worthy of mentorship" through initiative. To be one well, the mentoring literature warns against the named pitfalls: treating it as lip service, conflating it with coaching, persisting in a mismatched relationship, and "expecting mentors to force growth on unwilling mentees." The through-line is that mentoring is relationship-first and mentee-driven — the mentor offers perspective, but the mentee must own the growth. A senior PM who mentors widely also compounds their own influence across the org, well beyond their direct reports.

Common failure modes

The failure modes cluster around a few predictable traps. The signature one is the hero-PM leader who keeps making the decisions rather than developing the people who should — the inversion of EMPOWERED's "developing people is job 1," and the habit that caps a team at the leader's own bandwidth. Its close cousin is the status-update 1:1: letting the protected growth conversation decay into project reporting, which the weekly-1:1 prescription exists specifically to prevent.

The remaining traps follow from skipping the primer's own disciplines. Mode confusion — coaching when you should be managing a real performance problem, or managing (evaluating) when you promised to coach — is the pitfall the mentoring literature flags as conflating the modes. Telling instead of asking forfeits the entire point of the facilitator stance. Ruinous empathy — withholding direct feedback to protect the relationship — is the exact gap Radical Candor names. And the boil-the-ocean growth plan ignores Cagan's discipline of limiting focus to a few critical areas. The meta-failure beneath them all is treating development as something you do when there's spare time, rather than the job itself.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • Organizing product teams — the structural counterpart: how you arrange the PMs you are developing into empowered, well-bounded teams.
  • Product leadership & the operating model — the broader leadership context in which "developing people is job 1" sits, including hiring and vision-setting.
  • Influence without authority — both a competency you coach PMs toward and the skill a leader uses to develop people they don't formally control.
  • Giving and receiving feedback — the deeper craft of SBI, Radical Candor, and the psychology of defensiveness that feedback frameworks manage.
  • PM competencies & career frameworks — the detailed competency models and leveling rubrics that growth plans are assessed against.
  • Performance management — the managing mode's harder edge: when development isn't working and the conversation shifts from coaching to accountability.

References

  1. mcleanonline.medium.com ·cited· (book-summary)

    Distilling Cagan's EMPOWERED: a product leader's primary responsibility is coaching, using a structured assessment to identify specific skill gaps and creating targeted coaching plans focused on a few critical areas rather than attempting broad transformation.

  2. mindtheproduct.com ·cited· (practitioner-canon)

    Mentoring is 'ongoing perspective, support, and guidance via an established relationship' distinct from coaching or managing, aimed at broader career development; key pitfalls are treating it as lip service, conflating it with coaching, continuing mismatched relationships, and expecting mentors to force growth on unwilling mentees.

  3. blog.logrocket.com ·cited· (practitioner-blog)

    PM mentorship fills the gap between theory and practice; the distinction is that mentoring focuses on personal growth and better decisions over time and empowers independent development, whereas coaching is more directive and immediate skill-building.

  4. coventures.io ·cited· (practitioner-blog)

    Effective PM coaching starts by understanding each person's strengths and gaps through discovery and structured assessment, then builds a personalized development plan that balances skill-building (strategy, communication) with mentoring on leadership across execution, customer insight, strategy, and influence.

  5. rework.withgoogle.com ·cited· (reference)

    Google's re:Work frames coaching as moving from 'fixer' to 'facilitator': managers practice active listening and ask open-ended questions to surface the team member's own insight rather than supplying answers, building independence.

  6. cultureamp.com ·cited· (reference)

    The GROW model has four sequential phases — Goal (what they want to achieve), Reality (the facts now), Options/Obstacles (ways to address it), and Will/Way-forward (chosen action) — that a manager uses to understand aspirations, assess the situation, explore solutions together, and set concrete steps.

  7. ccl.org ·cited· (reference)

    The Center for Creative Leadership's SBI model structures feedback as Situation, Behavior, and Impact — concrete observations rather than assumptions about intent — which 'reduces anxiety around giving feedback, as well as the defensiveness of the recipient in hearing it.'

  8. radicalcandor.com ·cited· (practitioner-canon)

    Kim Scott's Radical Candor rests on two principles, Care Personally and Challenge Directly; to deliver it well, use the SBI structure, rehearse with a peer, and follow HHIIPP — helpful, humble, immediate, in-person, public praise/private criticism, and about behavior not personality.

  9. ravi-mehta.com ·cited· (practitioner-canon)

    Ravi Mehta's framework defines 12 PM competencies in four areas — Product Execution, Customer Insight, Product Strategy, and Influencing People — and argues peak PMs don't excel equally at all of them but identify strengths, understand gaps, and build balanced teams with complementary skills.

  10. news.aakashg.com ·cited· (practitioner-canon)

    The PM ladder splits into IC and management tracks spanning roughly 5–9 levels from APM to VP; as PMs advance the focus shifts from execution at junior levels to vision and organizational impact at senior levels while foundational execution skills are retained.

  11. uservoice.com ·cited· (practitioner-blog)

    Scaling PM capability means establishing clear single-owner boundaries by product/role/domain rather than overlapping authority, and hiring for a mix of experience levels and complementary skills rather than cloning the first PM, with supporting roles freeing seniors for strategy.

  12. productplan.com ·cited· (practitioner-blog)

    A good PM mentor offers firsthand wisdom that accelerates learning beyond going it alone; to find or be one, look within your company and product communities, build genuine mutual-benefit relationships, and show you are 'worthy of mentorship' through initiative and commitment to growth.

  13. medium.com ·cited· (practitioner-blog)

    Applied in a PM context, the SBI model delivers clearer, judgment-free feedback by describing when/where a situation occurred, the specific observable behavior without assumptions, and its measurable impact — reducing anxiety and the tendency to take feedback personally.

  14. productledalliance.com ·cited· (practitioner-blog)

    The PM career path runs from APM to CPO with ~2–3 years per level; expectations intensify from learning customer empathy and basic prioritization, to strategic ownership and cross-functional leadership at senior levels, to executive vision-setting and org scaling above Director.

  15. lattice.com ·cited· (practitioner-blog)

    In 1:1s the GROW model has managers ask reflective questions through Goal, Reality, Obstacles, and Way-Forward rather than providing solutions, so reports discover their own path and build ownership of their development.

  16. andrewclark.co.uk ·cited· (book-summary)

    EMPOWERED holds that 'developing people is job 1' via weekly 1-on-1s (minimum 30 minutes) focused on growth not status, assessing members across product, process, and people skills, finding capability gaps against expectations, then limiting the coaching plan to three development areas.

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-15_mentoring-and-coaching-pms.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
